subject: Re: contrast: AREtomo vs WARP
body:
hi all,

to add to this thread.  

First, an update: this issue was resolved eventually by updating AREtomo. 
The problem was that the XF files output from AREtomo were all zeros.

However, we are again experiencing a similar issue on new data and can't 
figure out why.  I should first say that this WARP>AREtomo>WARP pipeline is 
now working well for myself and others in the lab, but one project it is 
failing on.  The main difference I can see is that his data is how it uses 
the K3 as a square detector rather than rectangular like others in the 
lab.  

Some key points:

   - AREtomo reconstruction looks perfect.
   - WARP reconstruction is basically noise and some large features are 
   visible moving throughout the entire tomogram as you slice through Z, 
   indicating to me that something is horribly wrong with alignments.
   - importing into WARP after AREtomo: all of the CTF fits look good in 
   WARP
   - the xf files look reasonable, similar to others in the lab.
   - the mdoc seems fine, and detector dimensions are square there.

Is there anything we might be missing here for why a K3 used as square 
detector would be so problematic?   Any other troubleshooting steps I can 
take to dig a bit deeper?  

thanks all

Jesse

On Friday, November 24, 2023 at 2:10:49 PM UTC+1 lorenzo...@gmail.com wrote:

> Hi all,
>
> There are many things at play here, so it's hard to tell what *might* be 
> the cause of the difference in your reconstruction.
>
> - patch alignments in aretomo are pretty nice (and introduce a 
> difference), but cannot trivially be converted to warp/M parametrization. I 
> tried to ask both Dimitry and Shawn about this before, and while probably 
> possible, it's not the kind of conversion you can quickly spin up with a 
> script. However, most people who use Warp/M for high resolution work say 
> that the alignment defects can usually be compensated by M, even if the 
> initial alignment only has global shifts/rotations, so I wouldn't worry 
> about that too much. I generally use "patch-corrected" tomgorams just as a 
> way to improve visualisation, but go back to warp/M afterwards.
> - CTF correction in Warp is 3D, while aretomo only introduce (2d?) CTF 
> correction in one of the latest versions. This will make a difference.
> - reconstructions will be centered/shifted/sized differently due to 
> several small different choices made in the code (i.e: how to round pixel 
> sizes when binning, what to consider the origin, ...)
> - I *think* there's a different filtering on the outputs in aretomo that 
> isn't there in warp, so the high res noise makes it harder to see features 
> in the warp tomograms. A simple gaussian filter helps a lot here. Denoising 
> as well.
> - aretomo might have fixed the tilt if you're working with `-TiltCor 1`, 
> so this can significantly change how the tomograms look side by side 
> because of a tilt difference. You can account for this in warp by updating 
> the mdoc files after doing the alignment in aretomo and before importing 
> them.
>
> Either way, I would recommend not using aretomo in its current state for 
> high resolution work, especially due to the CTF correction. I use it for 
> alignments and "preview" tomograms only.
>
> If you're interested, I have a big script whose purpose is to automate the 
> aretomo/warp pipeline with all the steps in between. It's not really in a 
> state where I would *recommend* other people to use it, but I use it 
> regularly and it made my life easier when dealing with all this: 
> https://github.com/brisvag/waretomo. Feel free to take a look and/or use 
> it!
>
> Best,
> Lorenzo
>
> On Fri, Nov 24, 2023 at 12:34 PM Mathew McLaren <cla...@gmail.com> wrote:
>
>> Hi Jesse,
>>
>> What is the command you're using for Aretomo? From my experience, the 
>> alignment of the tomogram should be the same, i.e. the central plane on 
>> both the Warp and Aretomo outputs should be the same field of view. If 
>> they're different, you might be asking Aretomo to do further alignments 
>> that Warp doesn't take advantage of. If you can run a barebones Aretomo 
>> command that gives the same alignments as Warp (but gives better contrast), 
>> you can use this for your particle picking as the positions should be the 
>> same. There was a post elsewhere on the mailing list where somebody 
>> substituted their deconvolved tomograms into the main reconstruction folder 
>> and ran template matching, you could probably do the same with your data 
>> (or use a separate program).
>>
>> If Aretomo is doing patches but Warp does not, then why would I ever use 
>>> warp? Presumably though through M it can compensate for this difference.
>>>
>> Yeah, the alignment from the XF file should typically be good enough that 
>> M can run its own improvements later on. I was told that if you can pick 
>> your particle well enough, you should be able to get to a good enough 
>> resolution that tilt series refinements will improve it. To my knowledge, 
>> if you wanted to use more alignment information than what the standard XF 
>> output provides, the only way is to use Imod with local alignments and 
>> process your data in emClarity instead. It would be nice if Warp could take 
>> advantage of local alignment information from Aretomo's .aln file or the 
>> Imod _local.xf files but I don't know if there's any plans for this to 
>> happen.
>>
>> Cheers,
>>
>> Mat
>>
>> On Fri, 24 Nov 2023 at 10:52, mrJ <bluu...@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>>> Hi all, thanks for the responses.
>>>
>>> Ik still a bit confused.
>>>
>>> If Aretomo is doing patches but Warp does not, then why would I ever use 
>>> warp? Presumably though through M it can compensate for this difference. As 
>>> suggested, I tried running without patches and it looks only a bit worse 
>>> than with patches.  Still looks much better than WARP.  
>>>
>>> Yes if I do deconvolution on the WARP output it looks better and 
>>> reasonable, but still the details are way less than the Aretomo recon. It's 
>>> a bit concerning, because I did template matching and subtomogram averaging 
>>> and it gave a very poor result. I'm trying to get to the bottom of it.
>>>
>>> One thing I noticed is that the tomograms do not look identical at the 
>>> same z section (they are off by 10-15 z slices). Is that also an expected 
>>> difference between warp and Aretomo? Or is that an indicator that something 
>>> is wrong. If this is not expected, any thoughts on what could cause this?  
>>>
>>> maybe it's just that WARP does CTF correction and ARETomo does not....  
>>> is htere any way to temporarily turn off WARP ctf correction for testing 
>>> this?
>>>
>>> thanks!
>>>
>>> Jesse Hansen 
>>>
>>>
>>> On Friday, November 24, 2023 at 11:24:12 AM UTC+1 ricardo...@gmail.com 
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Mat,
>>>>
>>>> You are right, Warp does apply CTF correction to full tomograms. I was 
>>>> confusing it with reconstruction in other programs. So that might explain 
>>>> the difference in contrast indeed.
>>>>
>>>> Thank you!
>>>>
>>>> --
>>>> Ricardo Diogo Righetto
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> Em qui., 23 de nov. de 2023 às 21:39, Mathew McLaren <cla...@gmail.com> 
>>>> escreveu:
>>>>
>>>>> Hi all,
>>>>>
>>>>> Just wanted to add a couple of comments to this. For Aretomo 
>>>>> reconstruction, if you run it without -Patch and -TiltCor then it should be 
>>>>> essentially identical to Warp's output. I'm assuming the XF files don't 
>>>>> change with the application of patches, although if that's not the case it 
>>>>> would be good to know. Also, I believe Warp does apply CTF correction to 
>>>>> the tomograms, or at least that's my interpretation of the methods section 
>>>>> in the paper (emphasis mine):
>>>>>
>>>>>> Warp takes the* local defocus and sample distortion, as well as 
>>>>>> magnification anisotropy into account when reconstructing full or partial 
>>>>>> tomographic volumes*. For a partial reconstruction at any position 
>>>>>> in the volume, the original 2D images are sampled at the following 
>>>>>> positions:
>>>>>>
>>>>>> where *R**Euler* is the rotation matrix for 3 Euler angles following 
>>>>>> the Xmipp convention55 
>>>>>> <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR55>, *α* is 
>>>>>> the stage tilt angle, *ψ* is the in-plane angle of the tilt axis, *p* 
>>>>>> is the particle position within the tomographic volume, and *o* is 
>>>>>> the in-plane offset of the tilt axis. The coordinates are centered within 
>>>>>> the volume and images. The CTF for each 2D image is calculated using a 
>>>>>> defocus of:
>>>>>>
>>>>>> where *z**Tilt* is the average defocus estimated for the tilt image, 
>>>>>> *n**Tilt* is the sample plane normal, *n**Tilt*,*z* is the z 
>>>>>> component of the normal and • denotes the scalar product between two 
>>>>>> vectors. *The reconstruction is performed in Fourier space using a 
>>>>>> gridding algorithm23 
>>>>>> <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR23>, with the data 
>>>>>> weighted by the respective CTF, and the dose- and tilt-dependent heuristic 
>>>>>> from RELION49 <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR49>, 
>>>>>> but without the final deconvolution step (that is the weights are inserted 
>>>>>> as |CTF|, not as CTF2).* To obtain a full tomogram, Warp 
>>>>>> reconstructs a uniform grid of small, cubical volumes with an overlap of 
>>>>>> 50%, and inserts the central 50% into the overall volume to account for 
>>>>>> artifacts associated with Fourier space reconstruction at the borders of 
>>>>>> the local volumes. This ensures the corrections can be applied with local 
>>>>>> precision and remain reasonably continuous between adjacent sub-volumes.
>>>>>>
>>>>> Cheers,
>>>>>
>>>>> Mat
>>>>>
>>>>>
>>>>> On Thu, 23 Nov 2023 at 16:08, Ricardo Righetto <ricardo...@gmail.com> 
>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>
>>>>>> One thing is yes, you should make sure that tilt series alignments 
>>>>>> are exactly the same between AreTomo and Warp for a fair comparison. And as 
>>>>>> Pablo said, if you do local patch corrections in AreTomo these will not be 
>>>>>> applied in Warp.
>>>>>>
>>>>>> Furthermore, Warp does not apply CTF correction to full tomograms as 
>>>>>> far as I know, so this would be the same (no CTF) in both reconstructions.
>>>>>>
>>>>>> My guess (and it's just a guess) is that each program filters the 
>>>>>> reconstructions in different ways, i.e. they apply different weighting 
>>>>>> schemes during backprojection.
>>>>>>
>>>>>> --
>>>>>> Ricardo Diogo Righetto
>>>>>>
>>>>>>
>>>>>> Em qui., 23 de nov. de 2023 às 16:03, Lorenzo Gaifas <
>>>>>> lorenzo...@gmail.com> escreveu:
>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hi all,
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Forwarding my own message to the list cause I just noticed I only 
>>>>>>> replied directly by mistake.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Best,
>>>>>>> Lorenzo
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> ---------- Forwarded message ---------
>>>>>>> From: Lorenzo Gaifas <lorenzo...@gmail.com>
>>>>>>> Date: Thu, Nov 23, 2023 at 3:08 PM
>>>>>>> Subject: Re: contrast: AREtomo vs WARP
>>>>>>> To: mrJ <bluu...@gmail.com>
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hi Mrj,
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> In my experience, this is typical (no filtering in warp), though it 
>>>>>>> might also be caused by the alignments being off (many steps along the way 
>>>>>>> could go wrong, tilt series ordering, angles, and so on).
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Do the features in the deconvolved version in warp look more visible 
>>>>>>> at least?
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> For a better comparison, you can also just apply a simple gaussian 
>>>>>>> blur to the warp reconstruction to get rid of the high res noise.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Best,
>>>>>>> Lorenzo
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> On Thu, Nov 23, 2023 at 1:36 PM mrJ <bluu...@gmail.com> wrote:
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> dear community,
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> I have included AREtomo as part of my WARP pipeline in order to get 
>>>>>>>> .xf file for alignment.  The AREtomo tomograms look quite nice, however 
>>>>>>>> when I use the .xf file to generate the WARP tomogram I notice the contrast 
>>>>>>>> is substantially worse.  See image below.  The tomogram looks otherwise 
>>>>>>>> reasonable, it just has really bad contrast.  For AREtomo I was also using 
>>>>>>>> WBP (which I think WARP does).
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Is this to be expected? is it because WARP has corrected CTF and 
>>>>>>>> therefore less contrast?
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> thanks!
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> [image: Screenshot 2023-11-23 at 1.35.02 PM.png]
>>>>>>>>
